assertions.expectation: Explains that GKE's maximum upgrade parallelism is approximately 20 nodes simultaneously, regardless of maxSurge setting
assertions.reason: Response does not mention the 20-node parallelism limit or explain how maxSurge settings don't increase actual upgrade parallelism

assertions.expectation: Calculates that at 20-node parallelism, upgrading 3,000 nodes requires ~150 batches — far more than an 8-hour window allows
assertions.reason: Response does not provide any mathematical calculation of batches or timing based on parallelism limits

assertions.expectation: Recommends splitting upgrades across multiple maintenance windows (weekends) rather than attempting all at once
assertions.reason: Response clearly shows a staggered upgrade schedule across multiple weeks and suggests extending to full weekend windows

assertions.expectation: Suggests upgrading CPU pools and GPU pools separately, prioritizing CPU pools first as they're less disruptive
assertions.reason: Response shows separate upgrade schedules for CPU pools (Week 1) and GPU pools (Week 2) with CPU pools going first

assertions.expectation: Recommends upgrading GPU pools during scheduled gaps between training/inference workloads
assertions.reason: While response mentions GPU pools, it doesn't specifically address scheduling around training/inference workload gaps

assertions.expectation: Suggests expanding the maintenance window or using multiple consecutive windows for large pool upgrades
assertions.reason: Response explicitly shows extending maintenance window from Saturday-Sunday and demonstrates multi-week staggered approach

assertions.expectation: Mentions that cluster autoscaler can only scale one node pool at a time — this interacts with upgrade scheduling
assertions.reason: Response does not mention cluster autoscaler limitations or how they interact with upgrade scheduling

assertions.expectation: Addresses the 15,000 node standard limit and notes GKE 1.31+ supports up to 65,000 for AI workloads
assertions.reason: Response does not mention any node limits (15,000 standard or 65,000 for AI workloads) or version-specific capabilities
